/eas/zoom/{fileId}/{hash}/obj_uuid/{obj_.description: The endpoint zoom is used to deliver tiles for images. Use the `/api/db` and `/api/search` endpoints to retrieve the base URL to zoom and add the parameters to pick the tile accordingly. Tiles can be of any size and position. The `avoid_interpolation` part of the URL is there for compabitlity reasons with easydb 5 and currently unsupported in fylr. This endpoint uses the default tile size `320`.

parameters.name: fileId
parameters.description: The ID of the file.

parameters.name: hash
parameters.description: The `hash` is the `file.hash` as described [here](https://docs.fylr.io/for-developers/system-data-types/file#hash).

parameters.name: version
parameters.description: The `file.class_extension` as described [here](https://docs.fylr.io/for-developers/system-data-types/file#hash).

parameters.name: obj_uuid
parameters.description: The `_uuid` of the object the file is linked into. This is used for the permission check.

parameters.name: x-fylr-signature
parameters.description: The signature to omit file permission checks. It will be added to the URL when retrieved through `/api/db`, `/api/search` or other endpoints which deliver fylr URLs.

parameters.name: zoom_viewport
parameters.description: The viewport is the width of the desired rectangle for the tile selection. The minimum is 100. If the original image width is 4000px and the viewport is 40000px, the zoom factor will be 10. The image is zoomed so that the width of the resulting image is as large as this parameter.

parameters.name: zoom_x
parameters.description:
Within the viewport zoomed image `zoom_x` is the tile number on the x-axis on
a tought grid. The tile size is 320 unless given otherwise. If an image is
3200px (width) x 16000px (height) and the viewport is 32000px, we have a zoom
factor of 10. With a tile size of 320, this results in a grid of 100 x 50
tiles.

parameters.name: zoom_y
parameters.description:
Within the viewport zoomed image `zoom_y` is the tile number on the y-axis on
a tought grid. The tile size is 320 unless given otherwise. If an image is
3200px (width) x 16000px (height) and the viewport is 32000px, we have a zoom
factor of 10. With a tile size of 320, this results in a grid of 100 x 50
tiles.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description:
The output format of a tile. The internal compute chain works with a `BMP` without alpha channel. So, the zoomer
will not support transparency for `PNG`, but use a white background instead.

parameters.name: obj_uuid
parameters.description: The `_uuid` of the object the file is linked into. This is used for the permission check.

/eas/iiif/{fileId}/{hash}/obj_uuid/{obj_.description:
Serve an image derivative for a file `version` per the
[IIIF Image API 2.0](https://iiif.io/api/image/2.0/): the requested `region`
is cropped, scaled to `size`, rotated by `rotation` and rendered as
`quality`.`format`. Reading the image requires `RIGHT_ASSET_SHOW`. The
`info.json` paths describe the parameters a given image supports.

200.description: The tile exists and is accessible. No response body.

403.description:
Access to the file is denied (`code` `InsufficientRights`):
the caller holds neither `RIGHT_ASSET_SHOW` nor
`RIGHT_ASSET_DOWNLOAD` (and no `deep_link` access applies), or
the request `hash` / `version` does not match the stored file,
or the `x-fylr-signature` is invalid or expired. This endpoint
returns no `401`; a missing or invalid token is treated as an
anonymous request.

404.description:
The addressed file id does not exist. A wrong `hash`, wrong
`version`, or an invalid / expired signature is a `403`, not
a `404`.

/eas/iiif/{fileId}/{hash}/obj_uuid/{obj_.description:
The base [IIIF](https://iiif.io/api/image/2.0/) resource URL for a file
`version`, with no trailing path segment. It carries no image itself: any
request is `303`-redirected to the corresponding `info.json`, where the
access rights are checked.

get.description:
A IIIF resource URL with no trailing path segment (with or without
a trailing slash) is `303`-redirected to the corresponding
`info.json`. No access check is performed at this step; the rights
are checked on the redirected `info.json` request.

parameters.name: fileId
parameters.description: The ID of the file.

parameters.name: hash
parameters.description: The `hash` is the `file.hash` as described [here](https://docs.fylr.io/for-developers/system-data-types/file#hash).

parameters.name: obj_uuid
parameters.description: The `_uuid` of the object the file is linked into. This is used for the permission check.

parameters.name: x-fylr-signature
parameters.description: The signature to omit file permission checks. It will be added to the URL when retrieved through `/api/db`, `/api/search` or other endpoints which deliver fylr URLs.

parameters.name: version
parameters.description: The `file.class_extension` as described [here](https://docs.fylr.io/for-developers/system-data-types/file#hash).

Location.description: The `info.json` URL for this IIIF resource.

/eas.description:
Fetch the [file](https://docs.fylr.io/for-developers/system-data-types/file)
renderings for several files at once. The file `ids` are passed as a JSON
array in the query; the response is a map keyed by file `ID`. A file is shown
if the user holds `RIGHT_ASSET_SHOW` or `RIGHT_ASSET_DOWNLOAD` on the original
or on at least one of its versions. There is no blanket login gate: an
unauthenticated request is served via the `deep_link` system user if deep
linking is enabled. Use `GET /eas/{fileId}` to address a single file.

Differs from easydb 5: easydb 5 requires an authenticated user on
`GET /eas` ("The user must be authenticated."); **fylr** has no login
gate here — a missing token is served as the anonymous `deep_link`
user, and a denied request is `403` (`InsufficientRights`), never
`401`.

parameters.name: ids
parameters.description: A json formatted array of file IDs. Example: `[1,3,4]`.

parameters.name: obj_uuid
parameters.description: The `_uuid` of the object the file is linked into. This is used for the permission check.

parameters.name: check_for_duplicates
parameters.description: If set, the fylr database is queried for duplicates of the file. The `_duplicate` property will be added to the response.

parameters.name: mapping
parameters.description: If `mapping` is given, the file's metadata is used to compile a `_mapped_metadata` object in the response. `mapping` can either be the `_id` of the mapping or the keyword `standard`. The `standard` mapping will use the object type (and pool i.A.) to determine the standard mapping.

parameters.name: objecttype
parameters.description: The object type for which the mapping is compiled.

parameters.name: mask
parameters.description: The `mask` the mapping is compiled for.

parameters.name: pool_id
parameters.description: The `pool_id` sets the pool id to determine the mapping if `standard` is requested.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: The format of the output. `standard` is the default and includes the `versions` property. `long` includes `metadata` and `versions`. `short` doesn't include neither. If an unknown format is given `short` is used.

parameters.name: mapping_recipe_configs
parameters.description:
Configuration for recipes used in the metadata mapping profile. The value must
be an JSON-encoded object. See collections for a details description of the
format.

200.description: The response contains the file infos in a map.

additionalProperties.description: The [file](https://docs.fylr.io/for-developers/system-data-types/file) rendering. The `key` is the file ID.

403.description:
The caller may not see the file: it holds neither
`RIGHT_ASSET_SHOW` nor `RIGHT_ASSET_DOWNLOAD` on the original
or any version (and no `deep_link` access applies). The `code`
is `InsufficientRights`. This endpoint returns no `401`; a
missing or invalid token is treated as an anonymous request.

/search.description:
Run searches over the indexed objects, or over a different domain selected
by the request `type` (`pool`, `collection`, `event`, `message`, `user`,
`group` or `acl`). `POST /search` takes a `SearchRequest` body; `GET
/search` takes the same request as a JSON string in the `BODY` query
parameter so the call can be cached. Both return a `SearchResponse` with the
total `count` and the requested page of `objects`, restricted to what the
requesting user may read.

Differs from easydb 5: the searchable domains are `pool`, `collection`,
`event`, `message`, `user`, `group` and `acl` (plus the default object
search). easydb 5 has no `event` domain, and **fylr** has no
`pool_management` domain (easydb 5 documents `pool_management` to search
pools with `bag_write`).

post.summary: Search for objects.
post.description:
Run a search and return the matching objects.

The request body is a `SearchRequest`. Its `search` property holds a list
of search elements that are combined into a single boolean query. Each
element has a `type` — one of `text`, `in`, `range`, `changelog_range`,
`complex`, `nested`, `geo_bounding_box` or `geo_shape` (`match` is a
legacy alias of `text`) — and a `bool` that decides whether it `must`,
`should` or `must_not` match. Elements of type `complex` and `nested`
group further elements, so arbitrarily nested queries can be expressed.

By default the search runs over the indexed objects. Set `type` on the
request to search a different domain instead: `pool`, `collection`,
`event`, `message`, `user`, `group` or `acl`.

Only objects the requesting user is allowed to read are returned. The
response reports the total `count` together with the `objects` of the
requested page and any requested `aggregations`.

parameters.name: x-fylr-authorization
parameters.description:
The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`. You can also use `authorization`
which does the same. `authorization` is looked at first.

parameters.name: authorization
parameters.description: The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`.

200.description:
The search ran successfully. The response contains the page of
matching objects and the total result `count`.

400.description:
The search request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies which validation rejected the request. The
schema below lists the stable codes and shows a concrete example.

A malformed request body or a structural query error (for example a
search element with a missing or unknown `type`, or an unknown
`bool`) returns a generic error envelope without one of these stable
codes — treat a missing or unknown `code` as a generic bad request.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The requesting user lacks a required system right — for example a
search with `type: event` needs `system.api.event[get]`.

get.description:
Same as `POST /search`, but the `SearchRequest` is passed as a JSON
string in the `BODY` query parameter instead of in the request body.
This makes the request a plain `GET` that can be cached by the client or
by intermediate proxies.

Combine it with `cache_seconds` to let **fylr** add a `Cache-Control`
header to the response.

parameters.name: x-fylr-authorization
parameters.description:
The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`. You can also use `authorization`
which does the same. `authorization` is looked at first.

parameters.name: authorization
parameters.description: The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`.

parameters.name: BODY
parameters.description:
The `SearchRequest`, encoded as a JSON string. The content is the
same as the request body of `POST /search`.

parameters.name: cache_seconds
parameters.description:
When greater than `0`, **fylr** adds a
`Cache-Control: max-age=<cache_seconds>, public` header to the
response so the result can be cached for that many seconds.

200.description:
The search ran successfully. The response contains the page of
matching objects and the total result `count`.

400.description:
The search request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies which validation rejected the request. The
schema below lists the stable codes and shows a concrete example.

A malformed request body or a structural query error (for example a
search element with a missing or unknown `type`, or an unknown
`bool`) returns a generic error envelope without one of these stable
codes — treat a missing or unknown `code` as a generic bad request.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The requesting user lacks a required system right — for example a
search with `type: event` needs `system.api.event[get]`.

/search/parse.description:
`/search/parse` accepts a query string written in **fylr**'s search query
language and turns it into a `SearchRequest`. The language is summarised
here.

**Terms.** A bare term searches the full text of all fields:

  * `"annual report"` — a quoted string; escape a `"` inside it with `\`.
  * `2024`, `-5`, `1.5` — numbers, integer or decimal.

**Field comparisons.** `<field> <operator> <value>` matches a single
field. A field name starts with a letter or `_` and may contain letters,
digits, `.`, `:` and `_` (for example `title`, `_pool`, `data.year`). The
value is a string, a number, a geo term, or the literal `null`. Operators:

  * `==` — equals, `!=` — not equals
  * `=@` — matches, `!@` — does not match
  * `=*` — contains, `!*` — does not contain
  * `=^` — starts with, `!^` — does not start with
  * `>` — greater than, `>=` — greater than or equal
  * `<` — less than, `<=` — less than or equal

Example: `title =@ "report" && year >= 2020`

**Boolean operators.** Combine searches with `&&` (and) and `||` (or),
negate a single search with `!`, and group with parentheses `( ... )`:

  * `(color == "red" || color == "blue") && !year == 1999`

**Sub-search.** `<field> ==? ( <query> )` matches `<field>` against the
objects found by a nested query, for example
`artist ==? ( country == "FR" )`.

**Geo terms.** A geo value compares a geo field against an area:

  * `[lat1,lon1][lat2,lon2]` — a bounding box given by two corner points.
  * `["gkw","syj"]` — a box spanned by two geohash prefixes.
  * `["gkw"]` — a single geohash prefix.

Whitespace between tokens (spaces, tabs and newlines) is ignored.

post.summary: Parse a query string into a search request.
post.description:
Parse a query string written in **fylr**'s search query language into the
`SearchRequest` structure that `/search` expects.

With `search` set to `false` (the default) the parsed `SearchRequest` is
returned, so a client can inspect or adjust it before searching. With
`search` set to `true` the parsed request is executed right away and the
search result is returned instead.

parameters.name: x-fylr-authorization
parameters.description:
The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`. You can also use `authorization`
which does the same. `authorization` is looked at first.

parameters.name: authorization
parameters.description: The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`.

200.description:
The query string was parsed. The response is the parsed
`SearchRequest` when `search` is `false`, or the search result when
`search` is `true`.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. More than one failure maps to
this status — the `code` field of the response says which one. The
schema below lists the stable codes and shows a concrete example.

A malformed request body returns a generic error envelope without
one of these stable codes — treat a missing or unknown `code` as a
generic bad request.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

get.summary: Parse a query string into a search request.
get.description:
Same as `POST /search/parse`, but the parse options are passed as query
parameters instead of in a request body.

parameters.name: x-fylr-authorization
parameters.description:
The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`. You can also use `authorization`
which does the same. `authorization` is looked at first.

parameters.name: authorization
parameters.description: The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The query string to parse.

parameters.name: objecttypes
parameters.description:
Restrict the search to these object types. Repeat the parameter for
more than one object type.

parameters.name: search
parameters.description:
If `true`, run the parsed search and return its result instead of the
parsed `SearchRequest`.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Maximum number of objects to return.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: Number of objects to skip before the first returned object.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: Render format for the objects in the result.

parameters.name: timezone
parameters.description: Timezone used when interpreting dates in the query string.

200.description:
The query string was parsed. The response is the parsed
`SearchRequest` when `search` is `false`, or the search result when
`search` is `true`.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. More than one failure maps to
this status — the `code` field of the response says which one. The
schema below lists the stable codes and shows a concrete example.

A malformed request body returns a generic error envelope without
one of these stable codes — treat a missing or unknown `code` as a
generic bad request.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

/search/point_in_time.description:
Manage point-in-time (PIT) search contexts. A PIT is opened by a search
that sets `point_in_time` and keeps a stable view of the index across
paginated requests. `DELETE /search/point_in_time` closes the PIT contexts
named by `pit_id` and frees the resources they hold in the search backend.

delete.description:
Close one or more point-in-time (PIT) search contexts. A PIT is opened by
a search that sets `point_in_time` and is used to keep a stable view of
the index across paginated requests. Closing a PIT frees the resources it
holds in the search backend; PITs that are not closed expire on their own
after their `keep_alive`.

parameters.name: x-fylr-authorization
parameters.description:
The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`. You can also use `authorization`
which does the same. `authorization` is looked at first.

parameters.name: authorization
parameters.description: The access token in form of `Bearer <token>`.

pit_id.description: IDs of the point-in-time contexts to close.

200.description: The point-in-time contexts were closed.

400.description:
The request could not be processed — typically a malformed JSON
payload or a failure from the search backend when closing the
point-in-time. The errors raised here are wrapped generic errors,
so the response uses the standard envelope without a specific
stable `code`.

/settings.description:
Unauthenticated endpoint reporting brief information about the running fylr
instance: `version`, `instance` name, build details, Elastic `index_names`
and license-derived `capabilities`. The current system status is returned in
the `x-fylr-status` response header, which makes this path usable as a
liveness/status probe.

get.description:
This settings endpoint is unprotected and delivers brief information about
the running fylr instance. It can also be used to check the current status
of the running fylr (no authentication required; suitable for
liveness/status probes).

Differs from easydb 5: the response has no `api` field (easydb 5 reports
an `api` API-version number); fylr exposes only the `version` family
(`version`, `version_release_date`, `version_full`).

200.description: Brief information about the running fylr.

x-fylr-status.description:
Current status of the fylr system:
  * `startup`: Set during startup of fylr. Should rarely be seen.
  * `reindex`: System is re-indexing. This happens after a `reindex_block` is used during a schema commit.
    This state is also reached briefly after a regular `reindex`.
  * `busy`: If too many db connections are open, the system response with `busy`.
  * `purge`: System is purging (the database is being reset).
  * `ready`: In this state fylr is ready to answer requests.

On a `200` settings response the value is in practice `ready` or
`reindex`. The status reflects a system-wide condition (also
applied by the global status middleware): `startup`, `purge` and
`busy` are handled by that middleware as a `503` service
unavailable before this handler runs, so they are not seen on a
`200` settings response.

config_name.description: The internal name of the instace, as set in the base configuration.

instance.description: The name of the instance as set in the yaml config file.

version.description:
The version of the server. Changed with every release, follows
semantic versioning rules.

version_release_date.description: If known, the release date of this **version**. <unreleased> if not yet released.

startup_time.description: UTC time of when the server was started.

external_url.description: The url this fylr is reachable under. This is configured in the fylr yaml.

build_commit.description: The GIT commit hash set at compile time.

build_commit_time.description: The UTC timestamp of the GIT commit hash the fylr was built with.

name.description:
A UID generated when the database is created. The name gets
re-created when the instance is purged (POST
**/api/v1/system/purgeall**). This name is used as prefix
for Elastic index names.

num_cpu.description: The number of CPU available for the Go runtime as reported by [runtime.NumCPU](https://pkg.go.dev/runtime#NumCPU).

index_names.description:
Index names as used in the Elatic indexing system. There is
two indexes for reading (BaseRead & ObjectRead) and two for
writing. New writing indexes are created when a reindex is
started. Once that reindex has finished, the read index is
deleted and the read index name becomes the write index name.
If these names are identical, the index is up to date.

version_full.description:
The full version string including build metadata
(`global.VersionFull()`).

solution.description:
Legacy field kept for easydb 5 backup/restore
compatibility. Omitted when empty.

capabilities.description:
The EFFECTIVE capabilities of this instance — what a
frontend can rely on to decide which features to offer.
How fylr derives each flag (license, edition, base
config, indexer features) is internal; raw license data
is not exposed here.

Differs from fylr before v6.34.0: `indexer_vendor`,
`indexer_version` and `indexer_geo_support` were
removed — `geo_support` now reports the effective value
and the indexer details moved to `/inspect/system/`.

auth.description: SAML and LDAP authentication is available.

ci_hub.description: The CI Hub client is available.

read_only.description: This fylr is restricted to read-only use.

geo_support.description:
Geo queries are available: the license allows geo
support AND the configured indexer supports it.

two_factor.description:
Logins on this instance can use a second factor:
two-factor authentication is licensed, enabled in
the base config and at least one method is usable.

/system/sendmail.description:
Sends an email through the fylr server's mail infrastructure, composed as
a multipart message with separate HTML and text bodies and optional
attachments. Each send writes an `EMAIL_SENT` or `EMAIL_SENT_FAILED` event.
Requires `system.sendmail` (or `system.root`).

post.summary: This endpoint sends an mail using the fylr server infrastructure. The mail is composed as multipart mail with separate HTML and text bodies. The user needs the `system.sendmail` or `system.root` system right. After sending an email `EMAIL_SENT` or `EMAIL_SENT_FAILED` event is written.

from.description: The _From_ address to use in the mail header.

to.description: The _To_ address to use in the mail header.

cc.description: The _Cc_ addresses to use in the mail header.

bcc.description: The _Bcc_ addresses to use in the mail header.

subject.description: The _Subject_ to use in the mail header.

html.description: The body of the mail in HTML format. You can reference attachements using the `cid:` semantic. Use the `cid:<URL>` as source for images. If the `cid:` is followed by an URL, the data is requested and attached. The `cid:<URL>` is then replaced with a filename referencing the attachment. If the URL begins with `fylr.externalURL`, the current access token is appended to the URL. With this, local file previews can be accessed and embedded in the email.

text.description: The body of the mail in TEXT format.

filename.description: Filename used for this attachment, set in the `Content-Disposition` header of the attachment multipart.

content-type.description: The `Content-Type` header of the attachment multipart.

bytes.description: BASE64 encoded binary data for the attachement.

200.description: The mail has been handed over successfully to the MTA (mail transfer agent)

400.description:
Sending the mail failed. This also writes an `EMAIL_SENT_FAILED`
event. See the `code` and `parameters.msg` for the underlying
reason.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user holds neither `system.sendmail` nor
`system.root`.

/system/sendmail/test.description:
Sends a test email to a single `to` address to verify the mail
configuration. Each send writes an `EMAIL_SENT` or `EMAIL_SENT_FAILED`
event. Requires `system.sendmail` (or `system.root`).

Differs from easydb 5: the dedicated `system.sendmail` right grants this
operation. In easydb 5 the equivalent `POST /api/v1/settings/sendmail`
required "an authenticated session with the `system.root` privilege".

post.summary: This endpoint sends a test email to one mail address. The user needs the `system.sendmail` or `system.root` system right. After sending an email `EMAIL_SENT` or `EMAIL_SENT_FAILED` event is written.

to.description: The _To_ address to use in the mail header.

200.description: The mail has been handed over successfully to the MTA (mail transfer agent)

400.description:
Sending the test mail failed. This also writes an
`EMAIL_SENT_FAILED` event.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user holds neither `system.sendmail` nor
`system.root`.

/system/share_link.description:
Shortens url paths and generates an url friendly share id to be accessed via
/share/<share_id>.
* The creating user is stored alongside with the share link. If that user is archived
  the system user `system:deleted_user`.
* There is no API to revoke an existing share link.
* Share links are removed only after they expire.

post.description: Create a new share link.

path.description: The URI path to shorten, must start with `/`.

expires_at.description: ISO date and time when the share link expires. Defaults to one year.

alias.description: Optional alias that can be used instead of the randomly generated short id to access the share link. Only charaters defined in [RFC 3968](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.3) are allowed.

200.description: New share link entry

path.description: The target path of the share link URL.

share_id.description: The randomly generated id that is used to access the link via <external_url>/share/<share_id>.

alias.description: The user set alias to access the link via <external_url>/share/<alias>.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the short link was created.

expires_at.description: Timestamp when the short link expires.

400.description:
The share link could not be created. The `code` field identifies
the specific validation failure — invalid path, invalid alias,
expiration in the past, or a collision with an existing link.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

/system/share_link/{id}.description:
Resolves a share link by its `id` (the generated short id or a
user-set alias) and redirects to the stored target path prefixed with
`fylr.externalURL`. An unknown or expired `id` yields
`code: ShareLinkInvalid`.

get.description: Redirect to url referenced by given id, if available

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Shortened hash or alias set by user

Location.description: Location is the stored path prefix with `fylr.externalURL`.

400.description:
No share link entry for the given id (or it has expired). The
response carries `code: ShareLinkInvalid`.

/system/status.description:
Reports system-wide statistics: stored-object counts per objecttype
and per basetype, index-queue and file-processing-queue depth, and
per-index storage sizes. The response also sets the `x-fylr-status`
header to the instance's current lifecycle status (one of `startup`,
`ready`, `purge`, `reindex`, `busy`). Requires `system.root`.

get.description:
Returns counts of stored objects (per objecttype and per
basetype), index-queue depth, file processing queue depth and
per-index storage sizes. Used by the inspect UI's status page
and by external monitoring.

The `x-fylr-status` response header carries the instance's
current lifecycle status word (one of `startup`, `ready`,
`purge`, `reindex`, `busy`) — not the build / version / capability
details.

Requires `system.root`.

200.description:
Object, index and file processing statistics. Sets the
`x-fylr-status` response header to the current lifecycle
status word (`startup`, `ready`, `purge`, `reindex`, `busy`).

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/errortest.description:
Returns a synthetic error (`code: Test`) so a client can verify it
parses fylr's error envelope correctly.

get.summary: Trigger a test error (for error-handling validation).
get.description:
Returns a synthetic error so clients can verify they parse fylr's
error envelope correctly. Surfaces under `code: Test`.

400.description: The synthetic error.

/system/openapi/spec.json.description:
Serves the OpenAPI specification for the v1 API as JSON, the same
document as `/inspect/apidocs/spec/spec.json` but mounted on the API
surface. Accessible to a user holding `system.root`, or to anyone
when the base config flag `system.openapi_spec_endpoint.active` is
enabled.

get.summary: The OpenAPI specification for the v1 API (JSON).
get.description:
Returns the same JSON spec as `/inspect/apidocs/spec/spec.json`,
mounted under the API surface.

Access requires the `system.root` right, OR the base config flag
`system.openapi_spec_endpoint.active` to be enabled — in which
case the endpoint is reachable without authentication.

403.description:
The caller does not hold the `system.root` right and the base
config flag `system.openapi_spec_endpoint.active` is not
enabled. The response carries `code: InsufficientRights`.

/system/reindex.description:
Drops the search index into a freshly created one and starts a full
reindexing run. The run executes asynchronously; the call returns once
it has been started. Pass `blockFrontend=true` to serve a maintenance
page on the frontend until the reindex completes.

Differs from easydb 5: the handler performs no rights check and needs
no enable flag. In easydb 5 the equivalent `POST /api/v1/settings/reindex`
required a `system.root` session and the server-config flag
`server.api.settings.reindex` set to `true`.

post.summary: Rebuild the search index from scratch.
post.description:
Drops the search index into a freshly created one and triggers a
full reindexing run. The reindexing work runs asynchronously on
the server; the call returns as soon as the run has been started.
The frontend can be blocked while the reindex is in progress so
users do not see partial results.

This operation performs no rights check in the handler today.

parameters.name: blockFrontend
parameters.description:
If `true`, fylr serves a maintenance page on the frontend
until the reindex completes.

200.description: The reindexing run was started.

/system/purgeall.description:
Wipes the entire instance: drops the search index, optionally purges
file storage, and resets the database to an empty state, then
re-initializes a fresh instance. This is a full factory reset, not a
cleanup of soft-deleted records. Restricted to the `system:root`
user and cannot be undone. Purging must additionally be enabled in
the configuration (yml `fylr.allowpurge` and base config
`system.purge.allow_purge`), otherwise the call is rejected with
`code: PurgeNotAllowed`.

Differs from easydb 5: holding the `system.root` right is not enough —
the caller must be the actual `system:root` user, otherwise the call
returns `code: PurgeSystemRootOnly`. In easydb 5 the equivalent
`POST /api/v1/settings/purgeall` required only "an authenticated session
with the `system.root` privilege".

post.summary: Wipe and re-initialize the entire instance (factory reset).
post.description:
Drops the search index, optionally purges file storage (base config
`system.purge.purge_storage`), resets the database to an empty
state and re-initializes a fresh instance. This destroys all
data — it is not a cleanup of soft-deleted records. Restricted to
the `system:root` user and cannot be undone.

Purging must be enabled in the configuration: both the yml
`fylr.allowpurge` and the base config `system.purge.allow_purge`
must be set, otherwise the call returns `code: PurgeNotAllowed`.

parameters.name: redirect
parameters.description:
Optional URL. If set, the server starts the purge in the
background and immediately responds with a `302` redirect to
this URL instead of waiting for the purge to finish.

parameters.name: set_password
parameters.description:
Optional new password for the `system:root` user, applied
after the instance has been re-initialized.

200.description: The purge completed.

302.description:
Returned when a `redirect` value is supplied: the purge runs in
the background and the response redirects to the given URL.

400.description:
Purging is not enabled in the configuration (yml
`fylr.allowpurge` and base config `system.purge.allow_purge`).
The response carries `code: PurgeNotAllowed`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The caller is not allowed to purge. `code: SystemRightRequired`
if the user lacks `system.root`; `code: PurgeSystemRootOnly`
if the user holds `system.root` but is not the `system:root`
user.

/system/backup/new.description:
Starts a new backup of the database, files and configuration, stored
in the configured backup location. The backup runs asynchronously:
the call records the backup row and returns its descriptor with a
`running` status while the archive is produced in the background.
Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

put.description:
Starts a new backup of the database, files and configuration. The
backup runs asynchronously: the call returns the backup descriptor
with a `running` status immediately, and the archive is produced in
the background and stored in the configured backup location.
Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

200.description: The backup descriptor.

400.description:
The backup could not be started. `code: FormatNotSupported` if
the requested `format` is not one of the supported formats;
otherwise a generic error (`code: ServerGeneric`), for example
when no backup storage location is configured.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/backup/list.description:
Lists the backups on file as an array of backup descriptors. Requires
`system.backup` (or `system.root`).

get.description: Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

200.description: Array of backup descriptors.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/backup/{id}.description:
Retrieve or delete a single backup identified by `id`. An unknown `id`
yields `code: BackupNotFound`. Requires `system.backup` (or
`system.root`).

get.description: Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

200.description: The backup descriptor.

404.description:
The backup id does not exist. The response carries
`code: BackupNotFound`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

delete.description:
Only a backup whose status is `done` or `error` can be deleted.
Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

200.description: The backup was deleted.

400.description:
The backup cannot be deleted in its current status (only `done`
or `error` backups can be deleted). The response carries
`code: DeleteNotSupported`.

404.description:
The backup id does not exist. The response carries
`code: BackupNotFound`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/backup/{id}/download.description:
Streams the archive bytes of the backup identified by `id` as
`application/octet-stream`. An unknown `id` yields
`code: BackupNotFound`. Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

get.description: Requires `system.backup` (or `system.root`).

200.description: The backup bytes.

404.description:
The backup id does not exist. The response carries
`code: BackupNotFound`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/location/new.description:
Creates a new storage location; the body `id` must be `0`. A driver
connection problem does not block the save — the location is stored
with a non-`connected` `status`. Requires `system.location` (or
`system.root`).

post.summary: Create a new storage location.
post.description:
Requires `system.location` (or `system.root`). The `id` in the
body must be `0` for a create.

200.description: The created location.

400.description:
The location could not be created. `code: LocationMustMatchID`
if the body `id` does not match the create (`0`);
`code: LocationReadOnlyCannotBeDefault` if a read-only location
is configured as a default location; other validation codes
(unknown driver, missing config, ...) surface their own `code`.
Connection problems with the configured driver do not block the
save — the location is stored with a non-`connected` `status`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/location/list.description:
Lists the configured storage locations as an array. Requires
`system.location`, `system.config`, or `system.root`.

get.description: Requires `system.location`, `system.config`, or `system.root`.

200.description: Array of locations.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system/location/{id}.description:
Retrieve, update (upsert — the body `id` must equal the path `id`), or
delete the storage location identified by `id`. Reading requires
`system.location`, `system.config`, or `system.root`; writing and
deleting require `system.location` (or `system.root`).

get.description: Requires `system.location`, `system.config`, or `system.root`.

200.description: The location.

404.description:
No storage location with the given id is configured. The
response carries `code: ResourceNotFound`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

put.description:
Saves the storage location under the id in the path (an upsert —
the body `id` must equal the path id). Requires `system.location`
(or `system.root`).

200.description: The updated location.

400.description:
The location could not be saved. `code: LocationMustMatchID`
if the body `id` does not match the id in the path;
`code: LocationReadOnlyCannotBeDefault` if a read-only
location is configured as a default location; other
validation codes surface their own `code`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

200.description:
The location was deleted. A delete for an id that is not
configured also returns 200 (no row is removed).

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks the system right required for
the operation (typically `system.root`).

/system.description:
System-administration endpoints for operating a fylr instance:
statistics and status, search-index rebuild, backups, storage
locations, share links, sending mail, and wiping the instance
(factory reset). Most operations require a `system.*` right (often
`system.root`).

/group.description:
Manage user **groups**. A group bundles a set of users together so
they can be addressed by the ACL system. Each group has an owner
(`_owner`), an optional set of ACL items (`_acl`), system rights
(`_system_rights`), auth-method group mappings (`_auth_method_group_maps`)
and IP/subnet filters (`_ip_subnet_filter`). Groups are stored in the
`group` table; create / update / delete take effect immediately on the
request transaction (there is no working-copy / commit step).

Creating, updating (`POST` / `PUT`) and deleting (`DELETE`) groups
require the `system.group` (or `system.root`) system right. Listing and
reading groups (`GET`) require no system right — the results are filtered
to the groups the current session may read (`bag_read`); a `system.root`
user sees all groups.

Differs from easydb 5: easydb 5 requires an authenticated session with
the `system.group` right to read groups. fylr requires no system right
on `GET`; an unauthenticated list request returns an empty array rather
than an error.

post.summary: Create or update one or more groups.
post.description:
The payload is an array of group objects. Whether a group is created
or updated is decided by `group._version`: a group sent with
`_version: 1` is created; a group sent with a higher `_version` is
updated and its `group._version` must equal the stored version + 1,
otherwise the request fails with a 400 `code: VersionMismatch`. An
update also requires the stored `group._id` (or a `lookup:_id` object
that resolves one). The response carries the saved groups with their
`_id` / `_version` populated.

Creating a group of `type: system` is rejected with a 400
`code: GroupTypeNotAllowed`; changing a stored group's `type` is
rejected with a 400 `code: GroupTypeChangeNotAllowed`.

`PUT /group` is an exact alias — same handler, same semantics.

Differs from easydb 5: in easydb 5 the HTTP method selects the
operation (`PUT` creates, `POST` updates). In fylr both methods run
the same handler and the operation is chosen per group by
`group._version`, so a single array may mix creates and updates.

200.description:
The groups were saved. The response carries the saved groups
with their `_id` / `_version` populated.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. Common causes and their
`code`: a `group._version` that is not stored version + 1 on
update (`VersionMismatch`); creating a `type: system` group
(`GroupTypeNotAllowed`) or changing a stored group's `type`
(`GroupTypeChangeNotAllowed`); deleting a `type: system` group
(`DeleteSystemGroup`); the instance being in read-only mode
(`ReadOnlyMode`). A malformed payload or a group id in the URL
that matches no stored group surfaces as `code: ServerGeneric`
(there is no dedicated 404 for a missing group).

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks a required right. Create / update
(`POST` / `PUT`) and delete (`DELETE`) first require the
`system.group` (or `system.root`) system right; missing it yields
`code: SystemRightRequired` with `parameters.rights` listing the
accepted rights. A non-root user is then checked further:
creating needs `system.group[create]` (and `[create_acl]` /
`[create_system_rights]` when `_acl` / `_system_rights` are sent),
editing an existing group's `_acl` / `_system_rights` needs
`system.group[edit_acl]` / `[edit_system_rights]` — these also
surface as `SystemRightRequired`. Per-group ACL failures use
`code: RightRequired`: `bag_read` on read, `bag_write` on update,
`delete` on delete; changing a group's owner additionally requires
`system.root`.

put.summary: Create or update one or more groups (alias for POST).

200.description:
The groups were saved. The response carries the saved groups
with their `_id` / `_version` populated.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. Common causes and their
`code`: a `group._version` that is not stored version + 1 on
update (`VersionMismatch`); creating a `type: system` group
(`GroupTypeNotAllowed`) or changing a stored group's `type`
(`GroupTypeChangeNotAllowed`); deleting a `type: system` group
(`DeleteSystemGroup`); the instance being in read-only mode
(`ReadOnlyMode`). A malformed payload or a group id in the URL
that matches no stored group surfaces as `code: ServerGeneric`
(there is no dedicated 404 for a missing group).

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks a required right. Create / update
(`POST` / `PUT`) and delete (`DELETE`) first require the
`system.group` (or `system.root`) system right; missing it yields
`code: SystemRightRequired` with `parameters.rights` listing the
accepted rights. A non-root user is then checked further:
creating needs `system.group[create]` (and `[create_acl]` /
`[create_system_rights]` when `_acl` / `_system_rights` are sent),
editing an existing group's `_acl` / `_system_rights` needs
`system.group[edit_acl]` / `[edit_system_rights]` — these also
surface as `SystemRightRequired`. Per-group ACL failures use
`code: RightRequired`: `bag_read` on read, `bag_write` on update,
`delete` on delete; changing a group's owner additionally requires
`system.root`.

get.summary: List all groups visible to the current session.
get.description:
Returns the groups the current session may read (`bag_read`); a
`system.root` user sees all groups. Groups the session may not read
are silently omitted — no 403 is raised, and an unauthenticated
request simply yields an empty list, not a 401. The result is paged
with `limit` / `offset` and can be narrowed to specific group types.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Maximum number of groups to return. Defaults to `1000`.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description:
Number of groups to skip before collecting the result page.
Defaults to `0`.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description:
Comma-separated list of group types to return. When set, only
groups whose `type` is in the list are returned. Valid types:
`easydb`, `system`. Example: `type=easydb,system`.

200.description: Array of all groups visible to the current session.

/group/{id}.description:
Read or delete a single group addressed by its `_id` in the URL. `GET`
returns the group as a single-element array (subject to the `bag_read`
ACL); `DELETE` removes it (subject to the `delete` ACL), but a group
whose `type` is `system` cannot be deleted and yields a 400
`code: DeleteSystemGroup`. Neither path requires the `system.group`
system right on read; `DELETE` does.

get.summary: Retrieve a single group by id.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The group's `_id`.

200.description:
The group, returned as a single-element array of group objects
(the same shape as the create / delete responses).

400.description:
The request could not be processed. Common causes and their
`code`: a `group._version` that is not stored version + 1 on
update (`VersionMismatch`); creating a `type: system` group
(`GroupTypeNotAllowed`) or changing a stored group's `type`
(`GroupTypeChangeNotAllowed`); deleting a `type: system` group
(`DeleteSystemGroup`); the instance being in read-only mode
(`ReadOnlyMode`). A malformed payload or a group id in the URL
that matches no stored group surfaces as `code: ServerGeneric`
(there is no dedicated 404 for a missing group).

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks a required right. Create / update
(`POST` / `PUT`) and delete (`DELETE`) first require the
`system.group` (or `system.root`) system right; missing it yields
`code: SystemRightRequired` with `parameters.rights` listing the
accepted rights. A non-root user is then checked further:
creating needs `system.group[create]` (and `[create_acl]` /
`[create_system_rights]` when `_acl` / `_system_rights` are sent),
editing an existing group's `_acl` / `_system_rights` needs
`system.group[edit_acl]` / `[edit_system_rights]` — these also
surface as `SystemRightRequired`. Per-group ACL failures use
`code: RightRequired`: `bag_read` on read, `bag_write` on update,
`delete` on delete; changing a group's owner additionally requires
`system.root`.

delete.description:
A group whose `type` is `system` cannot be deleted — attempting to do
so yields a 400 with `code: DeleteSystemGroup`.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The group's `_id`.

200.description:
The group was deleted. The response carries the deleted group as
an array of group objects (the same shape as the create / read
responses).

400.description:
The request could not be processed. Common causes and their
`code`: a `group._version` that is not stored version + 1 on
update (`VersionMismatch`); creating a `type: system` group
(`GroupTypeNotAllowed`) or changing a stored group's `type`
(`GroupTypeChangeNotAllowed`); deleting a `type: system` group
(`DeleteSystemGroup`); the instance being in read-only mode
(`ReadOnlyMode`). A malformed payload or a group id in the URL
that matches no stored group surfaces as `code: ServerGeneric`
(there is no dedicated 404 for a missing group).

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks a required right. Create / update
(`POST` / `PUT`) and delete (`DELETE`) first require the
`system.group` (or `system.root`) system right; missing it yields
`code: SystemRightRequired` with `parameters.rights` listing the
accepted rights. A non-root user is then checked further:
creating needs `system.group[create]` (and `[create_acl]` /
`[create_system_rights]` when `_acl` / `_system_rights` are sent),
editing an existing group's `_acl` / `_system_rights` needs
`system.group[edit_acl]` / `[edit_system_rights]` — these also
surface as `SystemRightRequired`. Per-group ACL failures use
`code: RightRequired`: `bag_read` on read, `bag_write` on update,
`delete` on delete; changing a group's owner additionally requires
`system.root`.

/user.description:
Manage user accounts. The required rights differ per operation:
listing and reading users need only an authenticated session (reading
the password hash via `include_password` requires `system.root`);
creating or updating users is governed by a per-user save-right check
(a user may save their own record, but changing a protected field of it
— email, `_acl`, `_system_rights`, `_password`, … — still requires the
corresponding right); deleting a user requires the `system.user` system
right (or `system.root`); and changing the password requires the
`system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`). Some
properties additionally require `system.root`.

post.summary: Create or update one or more users.
post.description:
The payload is an array of user objects. Users with `_id` set are
updated; users without `_id` are created. The response carries the
saved users.

Differs from easydb 5: `POST` and `PUT` are aliases and both create
and update; create vs. update is decided by the presence of `_id`. In
easydb 5 `PUT /api/v1/user` only creates, `POST /api/v1/user` only
updates, and an update must carry the current `_version`.

parameters.name: confirm
parameters.description:
Set to `ignore_password_requirements` to re-submit a request that
was answered with a `202` because a password did not satisfy the
configured complexity rules, saving the user anyway (see the `202`
response).

parameters.name: skip_constraints
parameters.description:
If `true`, skip email-format validation on the saved users.
Allowed only with `system.root`; without it the request fails
with a `400` (code `ServerGeneric`).

200.description: The users were saved.

202.description:
A password in the payload does not meet the configured complexity
rules. Re-send with `confirm=ignore_password_requirements` to save
the user anyway.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies the specific reason.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user is not allowed to perform the operation.
The exact condition depends on the operation:

  * `GET /user`: only when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` — code `SystemRightRequired`.
  * `GET /user/{id}`: when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or when
    a non-`system.root` requester neither owns the target user nor
    holds the per-user `read` ACL right — code `RightRequired`.
  * `POST` / `PUT /user`: when a non-`system.root` requester lacks
    the ACL right needed to create or modify a targeted user
    (linking/unlinking groups, writing another user, or changing
    an owner) — code `RightRequired`. Other save-right violations
    (for example modifying a protected field of your own record)
    are reported as `400`, not `403`.
  * `DELETE /user/{id}`: when the requester holds neither
    `system.user` nor `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or
    when a `system.user`-but-not-`system.root` requester lacks the
    per-user `delete` ACL right on the target — code
    `RightRequired`.
  * `POST /user/change_password`: when the requester lacks the
    `system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`) —
    code `SystemRightRequired`; or when the new password fails the
    configured complexity rules — code
    `PasswordRequirementNotFulfilled` (the `error` message carries
    the configured password hint).

409.description:
The user could not be saved because another user already has
the same email or login.

put.summary: Create or update one or more users (alias for POST).

parameters.name: confirm
parameters.description:
Set to `ignore_password_requirements` to re-submit a request that
was answered with a `202` because a password did not satisfy the
configured complexity rules, saving the user anyway (see the `202`
response).

parameters.name: skip_constraints
parameters.description:
If `true`, skip email-format validation on the saved users.
Allowed only with `system.root`; without it the request fails
with a `400` (code `ServerGeneric`).

200.description: The users were saved.

202.description:
A password in the payload does not meet the configured complexity
rules. Re-send with `confirm=ignore_password_requirements` to save
the user anyway.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies the specific reason.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user is not allowed to perform the operation.
The exact condition depends on the operation:

  * `GET /user`: only when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` — code `SystemRightRequired`.
  * `GET /user/{id}`: when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or when
    a non-`system.root` requester neither owns the target user nor
    holds the per-user `read` ACL right — code `RightRequired`.
  * `POST` / `PUT /user`: when a non-`system.root` requester lacks
    the ACL right needed to create or modify a targeted user
    (linking/unlinking groups, writing another user, or changing
    an owner) — code `RightRequired`. Other save-right violations
    (for example modifying a protected field of your own record)
    are reported as `400`, not `403`.
  * `DELETE /user/{id}`: when the requester holds neither
    `system.user` nor `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or
    when a `system.user`-but-not-`system.root` requester lacks the
    per-user `delete` ACL right on the target — code
    `RightRequired`.
  * `POST /user/change_password`: when the requester lacks the
    `system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`) —
    code `SystemRightRequired`; or when the new password fails the
    configured complexity rules — code
    `PasswordRequirementNotFulfilled` (the `error` message carries
    the configured password hint).

409.description:
The user could not be saved because another user already has
the same email or login.

get.summary: List all users visible to the authenticated user.
get.description:
Differs from easydb 5: listing requires only an authenticated
session, not the `system.user` `read` right. The filter parameter is
`group_ids` (easydb 5: `groupids`) and the default `limit` is `100`
(easydb 5: default and maximum `1000`).

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description:
Maximum number of users to return. Defaults to `100` when omitted
or not positive.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: Number of users to skip from the start of the result.

parameters.name: group_ids
parameters.description:
Comma-separated list of group `_id`s; returns only users that are
members of one of the given groups.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description:
Comma-separated list of user types to filter by (for example
`easydb`).

parameters.name: changed_since
parameters.description:
ISO-8601 timestamp; returns only users whose `_updated_at` is at or
after this time.

parameters.name: archived_since
parameters.description:
ISO-8601 timestamp, or the literal `null`. With `null`, only
non-archived users are returned. Otherwise returns users whose
`_archived_at` is at or after the given time.

parameters.name: include_password
parameters.description:
If `true`, each entry includes the stored password hash (for
backup tooling). Requires `system.root`.

200.description: Array of all users visible to the current session.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user is not allowed to perform the operation.
The exact condition depends on the operation:

  * `GET /user`: only when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` — code `SystemRightRequired`.
  * `GET /user/{id}`: when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or when
    a non-`system.root` requester neither owns the target user nor
    holds the per-user `read` ACL right — code `RightRequired`.
  * `POST` / `PUT /user`: when a non-`system.root` requester lacks
    the ACL right needed to create or modify a targeted user
    (linking/unlinking groups, writing another user, or changing
    an owner) — code `RightRequired`. Other save-right violations
    (for example modifying a protected field of your own record)
    are reported as `400`, not `403`.
  * `DELETE /user/{id}`: when the requester holds neither
    `system.user` nor `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or
    when a `system.user`-but-not-`system.root` requester lacks the
    per-user `delete` ACL right on the target — code
    `RightRequired`.
  * `POST /user/change_password`: when the requester lacks the
    `system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`) —
    code `SystemRightRequired`; or when the new password fails the
    configured complexity rules — code
    `PasswordRequirementNotFulfilled` (the `error` message carries
    the configured password hint).

/user/session.description:
Report the current session. Returns the access token, the
authenticated user, their groups, system rights, the resolved
configuration (defaults and base config) and the instance settings.
Requires an authenticated session, no specific system right; a request
with no (or an invalid) token is answered with `400` `InvalidToken`.

get.description:
Returns the access token, the authenticated user, their groups,
system rights, the resolved configuration (defaults and base config)
and the instance settings. Does not require any specific system right
beyond being authenticated. Unlike the other `/user` operations, an
unauthenticated request is rejected with `400` `InvalidToken` rather
than `401`.

200.description: The current session.

400.description:
No authenticated user — the request carried no token or an
invalid one. The `code` is `InvalidToken`.

/user/change_password.description:
Change the authenticated user's own password. The current password
(`password`) is re-confirmed and verified, the `new_password` must
satisfy the configured complexity rules, and on success the user's
existing OAuth2 tokens are invalidated. Requires the
`system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`).

Differs from easydb 5: changing the password is here a `/user`
operation; in easydb 5 it is `POST /api/v1/session/change_password`.

post.summary: Change the authenticated user's password.
post.description:
Changes the **authenticated user's** password. The current password
(`password`) must be supplied and is verified; it is always the
authenticated user's own password that is changed. Requires the
`system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`). The new
password must satisfy the configured complexity rules. On success, the
user's existing OAuth2 tokens are invalidated.

The request body is form-encoded (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
or sent as a JSON object; the fields below are read with `FormValue`.

password.description:
The current password, re-confirmed. Must be non-empty and
must match the authenticated user's current password.

200.description: Password was changed.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies the specific reason.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user is not allowed to perform the operation.
The exact condition depends on the operation:

  * `GET /user`: only when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` — code `SystemRightRequired`.
  * `GET /user/{id}`: when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or when
    a non-`system.root` requester neither owns the target user nor
    holds the per-user `read` ACL right — code `RightRequired`.
  * `POST` / `PUT /user`: when a non-`system.root` requester lacks
    the ACL right needed to create or modify a targeted user
    (linking/unlinking groups, writing another user, or changing
    an owner) — code `RightRequired`. Other save-right violations
    (for example modifying a protected field of your own record)
    are reported as `400`, not `403`.
  * `DELETE /user/{id}`: when the requester holds neither
    `system.user` nor `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or
    when a `system.user`-but-not-`system.root` requester lacks the
    per-user `delete` ACL right on the target — code
    `RightRequired`.
  * `POST /user/change_password`: when the requester lacks the
    `system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`) —
    code `SystemRightRequired`; or when the new password fails the
    configured complexity rules — code
    `PasswordRequirementNotFulfilled` (the `error` message carries
    the configured password hint).

/user/{id}.description:
Read or remove a single user identified by `id` (their `_id`). `GET`
returns the user as an array with one element (an unknown well-formed
id yields `400` `UserNotFound`, not `404`); `DELETE` deletes, archives
or restores the user according to `delete_policy`.

get.summary: Retrieve a single user by id.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The user's `_id`.

parameters.name: include_password
parameters.description:
If `true`, the response includes the stored password hash (for
backup tooling). Requires `system.root`.

200.description:
The user, returned as an array with a single element. When the id
is well-formed but no user exists, the response is `400` with code
`UserNotFound` (not `404`).

400.description:
The request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies the specific reason.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user is not allowed to perform the operation.
The exact condition depends on the operation:

  * `GET /user`: only when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` — code `SystemRightRequired`.
  * `GET /user/{id}`: when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or when
    a non-`system.root` requester neither owns the target user nor
    holds the per-user `read` ACL right — code `RightRequired`.
  * `POST` / `PUT /user`: when a non-`system.root` requester lacks
    the ACL right needed to create or modify a targeted user
    (linking/unlinking groups, writing another user, or changing
    an owner) — code `RightRequired`. Other save-right violations
    (for example modifying a protected field of your own record)
    are reported as `400`, not `403`.
  * `DELETE /user/{id}`: when the requester holds neither
    `system.user` nor `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or
    when a `system.user`-but-not-`system.root` requester lacks the
    per-user `delete` ACL right on the target — code
    `RightRequired`.
  * `POST /user/change_password`: when the requester lacks the
    `system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`) —
    code `SystemRightRequired`; or when the new password fails the
    configured complexity rules — code
    `PasswordRequirementNotFulfilled` (the `error` message carries
    the configured password hint).

delete.description:
Removes a user. System users (`system:root`, `system:everybody`, ...)
cannot be deleted, and the requester cannot delete themselves. The
behaviour is selected by `delete_policy`:

  * `delete` — hard-deletes the user record. Objects, pools, groups and
    share links owned by the user are reassigned to the system user
    `system:deleted_user`. Irreversible.
  * `archive` — sets `_archived_at` and pseudonymizes the user record
    (name, address, login and email are cleared or randomized per the
    group's pseudonymization configuration). The user's collections are
    deleted and cannot be restored. Irreversible.
  * `unarchive` — restores a previously archived user.

When `delete_policy` is omitted, the instance's configured default
policy applies; if that is `ask` (or the selection is ambiguous), the
endpoint returns a `202` prompting the caller to choose (see below).
The deleted, archived or restored user record(s) are returned.

Differs from easydb 5: the caller chooses the outcome explicitly via
`delete_policy` (`delete`, `archive` or `unarchive`), and an omitted
policy can yield a `202` prompt. In easydb 5 there is no such
parameter — the user "is _archived_ if there has been activity,
otherwise deleted", and there is no unarchive via this endpoint.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The user's `_id`.

parameters.name: delete_policy
parameters.description:
How to remove the user. Omit to receive a `202` prompting for the
choice (when the configured default policy is `ask`). See the
operation description for the semantics of each value.

parameters.name: confirm
parameters.description:
Carried by the `202` confirm button. The operative parameter when
resolving the `202` is `delete_policy`.

200.description: The affected user record(s), as an array of `UserApi`.

202.description:
No `delete_policy` was supplied (and the default is `ask`). The body
lists the allowed policies; re-send with the chosen `delete_policy`.

400.description:
The request could not be processed. The `code` field of the
response identifies the specific reason.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user is not allowed to perform the operation.
The exact condition depends on the operation:

  * `GET /user`: only when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` — code `SystemRightRequired`.
  * `GET /user/{id}`: when `include_password=true` and the
    requester lacks `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or when
    a non-`system.root` requester neither owns the target user nor
    holds the per-user `read` ACL right — code `RightRequired`.
  * `POST` / `PUT /user`: when a non-`system.root` requester lacks
    the ACL right needed to create or modify a targeted user
    (linking/unlinking groups, writing another user, or changing
    an owner) — code `RightRequired`. Other save-right violations
    (for example modifying a protected field of your own record)
    are reported as `400`, not `403`.
  * `DELETE /user/{id}`: when the requester holds neither
    `system.user` nor `system.root` (`SystemRightRequired`); or
    when a `system.user`-but-not-`system.root` requester lacks the
    per-user `delete` ACL right on the target — code
    `RightRequired`.
  * `POST /user/change_password`: when the requester lacks the
    `system.user.change_password` system right (or `system.root`) —
    code `SystemRightRequired`; or when the new password fails the
    configured complexity rules — code
    `PasswordRequirementNotFulfilled` (the `error` message carries
    the configured password hint).

/right.description:
Access the ACL definitions and the per-context **right presets**
used elsewhere in the API (collections, objects, pools, ...).

get.summary: List all right definitions known to this fylr instance.
get.description:
Returns the `Rights` document: the right catalog built from the
built-in `rights.yml`, plus the `system` rights contributed by
enabled plugins (named `plugin.<plugin>.<right>`), plus every saved
`object` and `collection` preset. The document is the same for any
authenticated user — it is not filtered per session.

Differs from easydb 5: there is no per-`context` filter on this
endpoint. easydb 5 documents `GET /api/v1/right[/<context>]` where
the list can optionally be filtered by context; fylr serves only
`GET /right` and always returns the full catalog.

200.description: The right catalog.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

/right/{context}/presets.description:
List the right presets defined for a `context` (`object` or
`collection`), or save presets for it. Each preset in the request
is created when its `_version` is `1` and updated when its `_version`
is greater than `1`.

post.summary: Create or update right presets for a context.
post.description:
Saves one or more presets. A preset whose `_version` is `1` is
inserted (a new `_id` is assigned if none was sent); a preset whose
`_version` is greater than `1` updates the existing preset with that
`_id`, and the sent `_version` must equal the stored `_version` plus
one (otherwise the request fails with 400 `VersionMismatch`). The
response carries the saved presets.

Supported `context` values are `object` and `collection`. Any other
context value is rejected with status 400 (a generic `ServerGeneric`
error).

Requires the `system.rightpresetmanager` right. Rejected with 400
(`ReadOnlyMode`) when the instance is in read-only mode.

Differs from easydb 5: saving presets can return a 202 confirmation
step driven by the post-save collection-ACL re-validation (re-send
with `allow_invalid_acl=true` or `background_invalid_acl=true`).
easydb 5 documents only 200/400/500 for this save and no
confirmation flow.

parameters.name: context
parameters.description: The context the presets apply to.

parameters.name: allow_invalid_acl
parameters.description:
When true, the post-save collection-ACL validity check does not
raise an error if applying the presets would leave some
collections with an invalid ACL; the save still persists.

parameters.name: background_invalid_acl
parameters.description:
When true, the collection-ACL re-validation is queued as a
background task instead of running synchronously, and the request
returns immediately. This is the value the 202 confirmation
button re-sends.

parameters.name: background_invalid_acl_timelimit
parameters.description:
Go duration (for example `30s`) overriding the synchronous time
limit for the collection-ACL re-validation before a 202 is
offered. The default limit is `30s`.

200.description: The presets were saved.

202.description:
The post-save collection-ACL re-validation needs confirmation
before the transaction can commit. Two cases raise this:

- the re-validation exceeded the synchronous time limit — re-send
  the same `POST` with `background_invalid_acl=true` to run it as
  a background task; or
- applying the presets would leave a collection with an invalid
  ACL and the session user may store that collection's ACL —
  re-send with `allow_invalid_acl=true` to persist the save and
  mark the affected collections invalid.

The 202 body carries the button that re-sends the matching
parameter.

400.description:
The request could not be processed (invalid payload, unknown
context, ACL validation failure). The right-check pipeline
rejects presets that reference unknown rights, unknown
right parameters, unaccepted parameter values, or non-unique
choices. An unknown `context` is rejected with code
`ServerGeneric`; a read-only instance with code `ReadOnlyMode`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks `system.rightpresetmanager`
(required for the preset save and delete operations).

get.summary: List right presets for a context.

parameters.name: context
parameters.description: The context to list presets for.

200.description: Array of presets.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

400.description:
The `context` path segment is neither `object` nor `collection`
(code `UnknownEndpointMethod`).

/right/{context}/presets/{presetId}.description:
Retrieve or delete a single right preset, identified by its `_id`
within a `context` (`object` or `collection`). A delete of a preset
still referenced by any right row returns 202 until re-sent with
`confirm=delete`.

get.description:
The matching preset is returned wrapped in a one-element array — the
response is always an array. An unknown `presetId` yields an empty
array `[]` with status 200, not a 404.

parameters.name: context
parameters.description: The context the preset applies to.

parameters.name: presetId
parameters.description: The preset's `_id`.

200.description:
The matching preset wrapped in a one-element array, or an empty
array if no preset matches the id in this `context`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

400.description:
The `context` path segment is neither `object` nor `collection`
(code `UnknownEndpointMethod`).

delete.description:
Deletes the preset with the given `_id`. Deleting an unknown
`presetId` is a no-op that still returns 200. If the preset is still
referenced by any `right` row that is either not tied to a specific
object or tied to a current (latest, non-deleted) object, the first
request returns 202 instead of deleting; re-send with
`confirm=delete` to confirm. On confirm the preset is removed and the
database cascade deletes every `right` row that references it
(including rows on archived object versions).

Requires the `system.rightpresetmanager` right. Rejected with 400
(`ReadOnlyMode`) when the instance is in read-only mode.

Differs from easydb 5: deleting a referenced preset returns a 202
confirmation step (re-send with `confirm=delete`) instead of
deleting outright. easydb 5 documents only 200/400/500 for this
delete and no confirmation flow.

parameters.name: confirm
parameters.description:
Set `confirm=delete` to confirm deletion of a preset that is
still referenced by a right row; without it such a delete returns
202 instead of deleting. The 202's button re-sends this parameter.

200.description:
The preset was deleted. Deleting an unknown `presetId` also
returns this acknowledgement.

202.description:
The preset is still referenced by a `right` row that is either
not tied to a specific object or tied to a current (latest,
non-deleted) object. Re-send the same `DELETE` with
`confirm=delete` to delete the preset; the database cascade then
removes every `right` row that references it (including rows on
archived object versions).

400.description:
The request could not be processed. A read-only instance rejects
the delete with code `ReadOnlyMode`.

401.description: No authenticated user. The request must carry a valid access token.

403.description:
The authenticated user lacks `system.rightpresetmanager`
(required for the preset save and delete operations).

/pool.description:
Manage **pools** — fylr's hierarchical containers for storage,
permissions and metadata defaults. Pools form a tree; each pool can
inherit ACL items and configuration from its parent.

Differs from easydb 5: a save that touches collection ACLs is
confirmed with the `allow_invalid_acl` / `background_invalid_acl` /
`background_invalid_acl_timelimit` query parameters instead of
easydb 5's single `collection_rights_policy` parameter.

Differs from easydb 5: there is no separate `system.rights_management`
right for updating rights-management attributes on the root pool —
every save is gated on `system.poolmanager` (or `system.root`) plus
the per-pool `bag_*` rights. (easydb 5 required `system.rights_management`
for root-pool rights changes.)

post.summary: Create or update one or more pools.

parameters.name: confirm
parameters.description:
Answer to the file-resync 202 prompt. When a save changes a
pool's watermark or caption, the originals in that pool must
be re-rendered. Resend with `confirm=resync` to schedule the
re-sync, or `confirm=skip` to save without re-rendering.
Omitting it on the first request triggers the 202 prompt.

parameters.name: allow_invalid_acl
parameters.description:
After the pool change, **fylr** re-checks the ACL validity of
collections affected by the pool's ACL. With
`allow_invalid_acl=true` the save is accepted even if some
collections end up with an invalid ACL; with the default
`false` such a case is returned as an error.

parameters.name: background_invalid_acl
parameters.description:
If `true`, the post-save collection-ACL re-check is queued as
a background task instead of running inline, and the request
